title: Laghu Atiyār — Short Confession of the Twelve Lay Vows' Transgressions
summary: This is the short 'atichār' — a structured self-examination in which a Jain layperson (śrāvaka) reviews the twelve vows of householder life, one by one, and confesses any small slips made against each. For every vow it names the vow, recalls its agreed limits, lists the five recognized ways that vow can be subtly broken, and then asks forgiveness for whatever fault occurred 'by mind, speech, and body' with the phrase micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ ('may my wrongdoing be in vain'). It is recited in the daily Devasi (evening) and Rāi (dawn) pratikramaṇa so that the day's or night's lapses are acknowledged and released rather than carried forward.
ritualPlacement: Laghu Atiyār is the text of the fourth Āvaśyaka — Paḍikkamaṇa (Pratikramaṇa, reflection and atonement) — in its SHORT form, used in the daily Daivasika (Devasi) and Rātrika (Rāi) pratikramaṇa. In the Devasi flow it follows the Dvādaśāvarta Vāndaṇā (third Āvaśyaka) and precedes the Muni Vandanā and the sajjhāy cluster. It is the everyday counterpart of the extended bruhad-atiyar, which replaces it in the Pākṣika (Pakkhī), Cāturmāsika (Cāumāsī) and Sāṁvatsarika pratikramaṇa. The bracketed rubric inside the verses — 'if it is night, say rātri-sambandhī (concerning the night)' — is itself the vidhi (procedural cue) that adapts the same text between the Devasi and Rāi recitation.
authorAndSource: Traditional liturgical text of the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha Gaccha) Śvetāmbara Mūrtipūjaka tradition, as printed in the authoritative edition 'Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit' (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.). It is a composite in mixed Ardhamāgadhī/Prakrit and classical Gujarati: the technical names of the vows and their atichārs are preserved in their Prakrit forms, while the connective declaration ('I examine the five atichārs… all of it micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ') is in Gujarati. No single individual author is named for the short atichār in the source book; the underlying doctrinal scheme of the twelve vows and their five-fold atichārs derives from the canonical śrāvaka-ācāra tradition (e.g. the Upāsakadaśāḥ / Tattvārtha lineage). — Specific compiler unknown.
structureArc: The recitation is a fixed litany that walks down the householder's vows in canonical order. (Opening, the 'icchaṁ' paragraph): samyaktva (right faith) is affirmed first and its five atichārs — śaṅkā, kāṅkṣā, vitigicchā, para-pākhaṇḍī-praśaṁsā, para-pākhaṇḍī-saṁstava — are examined. Then the five aṇuvrata: v.1 prāṇātipāta-viramaṇa (non-violence); v.2 sthūla-mṛṣāvāda-viramaṇa (truth); v.3 sthūla-adattādāna-viramaṇa (non-stealing); v.4 śīlavrata (chastity / svadāra-santoṣa); v.5 parigraha-parimāṇa (limiting possessions). Then the three guṇavrata: v.6 diś-vrata (limiting the directions of travel); v.7 bhogopabhoga-vrata (limiting consumables and enjoyables) — which uniquely also enumerates the fifteen karmādāna (forbidden livelihoods); v.8 anarthadaṇḍa-viramaṇa (avoiding purposeless harm). Then the four śikṣāvrata: v.9 sāmāyika-vrata (periodic equanimity); v.10 deśāvagāśika-vrata (further restricting one's area for a day); v.11 pauṣadha-vrata (living monk-like for a day); v.12 atithi-saṁvibhāga-vrata (sharing food with ascetics). v.13 adds the five atichārs of saṁlekhanā (the end-of-life fast). The litany then sums all faults as the book's total of 'eighty-five atichārs' (samyaktva 5 + twelve vows 60 + fifteen karmādāna 15 + saṁlekhanā 5 = 85) and closes with a comprehensive micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ covering even errors of pronunciation (a missing or extra mātrā, anusvāra, word, or syllable). A final deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna tag (deśāvagāśiyaṁ… vosirāmi) renews the day's renunciation.
recurringImagery: This sutra is not built on poetic metaphor but on a single repeating liturgical formula, which is explained here once so the per-verse notes need not restate it. For every vow the pattern is: (1) NAME and number the vow; (2) recall its niyama — the specific limit the layperson has personally undertaken, often paired with jayaṇā (yatanā, careful exemption for unavoidable subtle harm, e.g. 'a rule against intentional killing of mobile beings, with jayaṇā in necessary activity'); (3) the refrain 'e… vratataṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ' ('I examine the five atichārs of this vow'); (4) the five named atichārs in Prakrit; (5) the closing release 'among these five atichārs, whatever transgression occurred concerning the day (or night) — all of it, done by mind, speech and body — micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ.' The number FIVE is the organizing motif: each vow has exactly five recognized atichārs, marking the boundary zone between keeping and breaking a vow. The threefold instrument 'mane, vacane, kāyāe' (by mind, speech, body) and the sealing phrase micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ recur after every block and are the heartbeat of the whole confession.
keyThemes: Self-examination (ālocanā) of the householder's twelve vows
Atichāra: the five subtle ways each vow is partially broken
Confession and release through micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ
The graded scheme: 5 aṇuvrata + 3 guṇavrata + 4 śikṣāvrata
Samyaktva (right faith) as the root preceding all vows
Jayaṇā / yatanā: conscientious care for unavoidable subtle harm
The fifteen karmādāna (forbidden livelihoods) under the bhogopabhoga vow
Saṁlekhanā: examining faults of the end-of-life practice
Daily renewal in the Devasi and Rāi pratikramaṇa

glossary.definition: A 'transgression' — a partial or subtle violation that strains a vow without wholly breaking it; each of the twelve vows has five. (Note: this book spells it અતિયાર, not અતિચાર.)
'May my wrongdoing be in vain / may it come to nothing' — the standard Jain formula of confession and seeking forgiveness that seals each section.
A Jain layperson (householder devotee); the feminine is śrāvikā. The subject who undertakes and reviews these vows.
The 'twelve vows' of the Jain householder: five aṇuvrata, three guṇavrata, and four śikṣāvrata.
The five 'lesser vows' — limited forms (suited to lay life) of non-violence, truth, non-stealing, chastity, and non-possession.
The three 'merit/enhancing vows' (diś-vrata, bhogopabhoga, anarthadaṇḍa) that reinforce the aṇuvrata by limiting scope and waste.
The four 'disciplinary/training vows' (sāmāyika, deśāvagāśika, pauṣadha, atithi-saṁvibhāga) that rehearse ascetic life.

Right faith / correct world-view; affirmed at the outset because it is the root that gives the vows their value. Here called samakita; its own five atichārs include doubt (śaṅkā) and craving (kāṅkṣā).
Sanskrit yatanā — conscientious carefulness; the prudent exemption a layperson allows for subtle, unavoidable harm in necessary activity, distinct from intentional violation.
The specific personal limit or resolve a layperson sets within a vow (e.g. the exact extent of possessions or directions permitted).
The fifteen highly harmful livelihoods (e.g. trade in charcoal, forests, animals, poison) a śrāvaka avoids; enumerated under the seventh vow.
The voluntary, gradual ritual fast undertaken at life's end; its five atichārs (e.g. wishing for this world or the next) are examined in v.13.
'Turning back' — the rite of reflection, confession and renewal; this atichār is its fourth-Āvaśyaka core.
Graded degrees of fault named in the closing summary: from a mere transgressive impulse (atikrama) and overstepping (vyatikrama) up to outright wrong conduct (anāchāra).
The tenth vow — restricting one's permitted area/activities for a fixed shorter period (e.g. a day); its renewing pratyākhyāna closes the recitation.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this as a checklist of conscience: it simply walks through the twelve commitments of Jain lay life and asks pardon for small failures in each — focus on the repeating rhythm 'name the vow → its five slips → micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ.' An advanced practitioner should attend to the precise Prakrit atichār-terms of each vow and to the jayaṇā clauses, which encode the exact doctrinal boundary between permitted necessity and culpable transgression.

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Authoritative text, spelling (અતિયાર), ritual placement of the short atichār in Devasi/Rāi, and the 85-atichār total.

sources.title: Jainpedia / Jain Śrāvaka-ācāra: the twelve vows of the householder (aṇuvrata, guṇavrata, śikṣāvrata) and their atichāras
sources.consultedFor: Standard classification of the twelve lay vows and the five-fold atichāra scheme.

notes: The source book names no individual author for the short atichār; the compiler is treated as traditional. The numbering shown in the verses is the book's pagination/structuring (the parenthetical page markers), not a verse meter — this text is liturgical prose with embedded Prakrit lists rather than metrical gāthās (except the one śloka-form Prakrit verse 'sacitta davva viggai…' under v.7). The book's total of 'eighty-five atichārs' is reached as samyaktva (5) + twelve vows (12×5=60) + fifteen karmādāna (15) + saṁlekhanā (5). Spellings such as અતિયાર and any doubled/single conjuncts are preserved verbatim from the book and should not be normalized. The bracketed Devasi/Rāi substitution cue is vidhi (stage-direction), not part of the spoken confession proper. URLs left blank where a stable citable link was not verified, per the no-hallucinated-citation rule.
